title: API Rate Limiting False Positives Blocking Legitimate Traffic
symptoms: 429 Too Many Requests responses for API consumers well below their rate limits
Rate limiter Redis counters showing inflated request counts
Redis node clock showing 3-minute drift after restart
Sliding window algorithm producing incorrect calculations due to time skew
Only requests processed by the restarted Redis node affected
root_cause: Redis node clock drift of 3 minutes after restart caused the sliding window rate limiting algorithm to miscalculate, counting requests from overlapping time windows and inflating rate counts
resolution: Synchronized Redis node clock via NTP, reset all rate limiting counters, and implemented time-independent rate limiting algorithm using token bucket instead of sliding window
preventive_actions: Implement NTP synchronization monitoring for all Redis nodes
Migrate rate limiting from sliding window to token bucket algorithm
Add rate limiter accuracy monitoring comparing actual vs measured rates
Implement rate limiter bypass for known partners during clock drift events
Create automated rate counter reset procedure for clock drift detection

timeline.event: Redis rate limiter node restarts with clock drift
Rate limiting counters begin showing inflated values
First partner reports receiving unexpected 429 errors
Jenny Liu investigates, discovers clock drift on Redis node
429 false positives stop, traffic flowing normally

embedding_summary: API rate limiter false positives from Redis clock drift causing sliding window algorithm to inflate request counts. 25% of legitimate traffic blocked with 429 errors for 30 minutes. 35 partners affected. Resolved by clock sync, counter reset, and migration to token bucket algorithm.
